news_summary: Football brings joy and community spirit to Gaza camps despite challenging circumstances.
news_summary_fr: Malgré des conditions difficiles, le football apporte joie et esprit communautaire dans les camps de Gaza.
news_summary_es: El fútbol aporta alegría y espíritu de comunidad a los campamentos de Gaza, a pesar de las difíciles circunstancias.

articles.title: Borrowed boots, borrowed joy: Football thrives in Gaza camps
articles.summary: Matches organised by a former professional player are providing a brief respite from the harsh reality of life for the thousands living in overcrowded tents, schools or damaged buildings in the shattered Occupied Palestinian Territory of Gaza.
articles.content:
In the Al-Mawasi area west of Khan Younis, where tents stretch across the sand and snaking queues form for water and food, Asaad Al-Azzabi prepares for a match a world away from what he once knew.

Before the war, Mr. Al-Azzabi played for Al-Tajammu Club in Rafah, where he and his teammates had access to pitches, training halls, coaches and equipment.

UN News

Borrowed boots

Now, he’s lucky if he can find boots to play in. “Sometimes I borrow a pair from a friend or patch them up with tape,” he says.

His home is now a tent in Al-Rahma Camp, a shelter for people displaced from Rafah, where access to clean water and sanitation services is scarce. He lives alone, after his wife left for Jordan with their son, who has cancer, to seek treatment.

According to UN data, around 1.7 million people are living in around 1,600 displacement sites across the Gaza Strip, most of them in temporary or informal locations. Most residents rely on water brought in by truck and are forced to cope with restrictions on the entry of equipment, fuel and repair materials.

Amid the struggle to meet basic needs, Mr. Al-Azzabi is preparing for the match with nearby Sheikh Al-Eid Camp. He explains the game plan to his players by drawing on the sand, before the team sets off on foot toward a pitch located among the tents of displaced people.

The match appears to be more than a sporting activity – it is a respite from the daily hardships of life in the camps.

Children and young men gather around the sandy pitch, applauding players, some of whom arrived after spending hours standing in queues for food, water or battery charging.

UN News

Something out of nothing

Referee Alaa Abu Taha, a referee with the Palestinian Football Association and a displaced resident of Rafah, says football has become the “only outlet” for many people in Gaza.

“With the most limited resources, we try to play. Now there is no sports infrastructure. The pitch we are standing on now was originally prepared for basketball and volleyball, but our people create everything out of nothing,” he says.

Gaza’s sports sector has suffered widespread destruction since the outbreak of the war. According to the Palestinian Football Association, hundreds of athletes have been killed, including many footballers, while hundreds of sports facilities have been damaged or destroyed, including pitches, club headquarters and training halls.

In Al-Mawasi these losses have not prevented players from organising a championship between displacement camps.

The big match

The match kicks off in front of a small crowd of displaced spectators, with Mr. Al-Azzabi taking part in boots held together by plastic tape. At the end of the match, Al-Rahma Camp defeats Sheikh Al-Eid Camp 2–1.

UN News

After the final whistle, young men from the camp lift him and his teammates onto their shoulders, while children and young people celebrate among the tents. For a few brief moments, the sound of displacement recedes from the scene, and football emerges as a rare space for joy.

“Under these difficult circumstances, to be able to come out and play a match like this is a very good thing,” says Mr. Al-Azzabi. “Congratulations to our camp. I dedicate this championship to my wife and son in Jordan, and I wish my son a speedy recovery.”

For him, the game is more than a sporting victory. It is a message to his distant family and an attempt to preserve what remains of his life as a former player, chasing the ball as if it were the last thing connecting him to who he was before the war.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights how football matches organized by displaced people in Gaza camps provide a meaningful respite and emotional uplift amid harsh living conditions caused by war and displacement. It shows community resilience and the preservation of hope and joy despite adversity, benefiting a large population in difficult circumstances. The story is focused, detailed, and illustrates a positive social impact beyond trivial or niche interest.
articles.personality_title: Football Brings Hope to Displaced Families in Gaza Camps
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people in Gaza live in crowded tents and damaged buildings after war forced them from their homes. Basic needs like water and food are hard to get, and sports facilities have been destroyed.

**What happened** – Asaad Al-Azzabi, a former professional football player, organizes matches between camps where displaced people live. Using borrowed or repaired boots and makeshift pitches, players come together to play games despite limited resources. Recently, a match between Al-Rahma and Sheikh Al-Eid camps ended with Al-Rahma winning 2–1.

**Impact** – These football matches offer a break from daily struggles and bring joy to people living in tough conditions. The games create a sense of community and normalcy, helping players and spectators forget their hardships for a while. For Al-Azzabi, playing football is also a way to stay connected to his past and send a hopeful message to his family.

**What's next step** – Organizers hope to keep holding these matches and possibly create more events that bring displaced people together. Continued support and access to sports equipment could help strengthen these efforts and improve life in the camps.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In Gaza’s harsh conditions, football matches organized by displaced residents provide a rare and meaningful moment of joy and connection.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le football apporte de l'espoir aux familles déplacées dans les camps de Gaza
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes à Gaza vivent dans des tentes surpeuplées et des bâtiments endommagés après avoir été chassées de chez elles par la guerre. L'accès à l'eau et à la nourriture est difficile, et les installations sportives ont été détruites.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Asaad Al-Azzabi, un ancien joueur de football professionnel, organise des matchs entre les camps où vivent les personnes déplacées. Avec des chaussures empruntées ou réparées et des terrains improvisés, les joueurs se rassemblent pour jouer malgré le manque de ressources. Récemment, un match entre les camps d'Al-Rahma et de Sheikh Al-Eid s'est terminé par une victoire 2-1 d'Al-Rahma.

**Impact** – Ces matchs de football offrent une pause dans les difficultés quotidiennes et apportent de la joie aux habitants vivant dans des conditions difficiles. Les jeux créent un sentiment de communauté et de normalité, aidant joueurs et spectateurs à oublier leurs souffrances un moment. Pour Al-Azzabi, jouer au football est aussi un moyen de rester connecté à son passé et d'envoyer un message d'espoir à sa famille.

**Prochaine étape** – Les organisateurs espèrent continuer à tenir ces matchs et peut-être créer plus d'événements qui rassemblent les personnes déplacées. Un soutien continu et un meilleur accès au matériel sportif pourraient renforcer ces efforts et améliorer la vie dans les camps.

**En une phrase** – Dans les conditions difficiles de Gaza, les matchs de football organisés par les déplacés offrent un rare moment de joie et de lien.
articles.personality_title_es: El fútbol lleva esperanza a las familias desplazadas en los campamentos de Gaza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas en Gaza viven en tiendas abarrotadas y edificios dañados después de que la guerra los obligara a abandonar sus hogares. Es difícil conseguir agua y comida, y las instalaciones deportivas han sido destruidas.

**Qué pasó** – Asaad Al-Azzabi, un exfutbolista profesional, organiza partidos entre los campamentos donde viven personas desplazadas. Usando botas prestadas o reparadas y canchas improvisadas, los jugadores se reúnen para jugar a pesar de los recursos limitados. Recientemente, un partido entre los campamentos Al-Rahma y Sheikh Al-Eid terminó con la victoria de Al-Rahma 2-1.

**Impacto** – Estos partidos de fútbol ofrecen un descanso de las dificultades diarias y traen alegría a quienes viven en condiciones duras. Los juegos crean un sentido de comunidad y normalidad, ayudando a jugadores y espectadores a olvidar sus problemas por un tiempo. Para Al-Azzabi, jugar fútbol también es una forma de mantenerse conectado con su pasado y enviar un mensaje de esperanza a su familia.

**Próximo paso** – Los organizadores esperan continuar realizando estos partidos y posiblemente crear más eventos que unan a las personas desplazadas. Un apoyo continuo y mejor acceso a equipo deportivo podría fortalecer estos esfuerzos y mejorar la vida en los campamentos.

**Una frase resumen** – En las duras condiciones de Gaza, los partidos de fútbol organizados por desplazados ofrecen un raro y valioso momento de alegría y conexión.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of worn, tape-patched football boots resting on sandy ground surrounded by simple tents arranged like a makeshift stadium, with shadowy silhouettes of players mid-action and a small, joyful crowd of abstract figures gathered nearby, all rendered in warm, earthy tones to evoke resilience and hope amid hardship.
